html:
Why Hail Claims in the Permian Basin Come Down to Documentation

Hail is the peril that does the most damage to commercial roofs across the Permian Basin, and it isn't rare here — this part of West Texas sits in one of the more hail-prone corridors in the country, and Odessa's building stock takes hits nearly every storm season. The trouble with a hail claim isn't usually proving a storm happened. It's proving what the storm actually did, because a lot of hail damage doesn't announce itself the way a torn-off section of roof does.

What Hail Damage Actually Looks Like on Different Roof Systems

On exposed-fastener metal panels, common across the pipe yards and fabrication shops around here, hail dents the panel surface and can crack paint coating or fracture the metal at fastener points, opening a path for water that isn't obvious from the ground. On TPO, PVC, and EPDM membranes, hail bruises the material — softening or fracturing its internal structure without puncturing it — and that bruised spot becomes brittle and prone to cracking weeks or months after the storm, long after anyone's thinking about it. On built-up and modified bitumen roofs, hail knocks granules loose from the cap sheet, exposing the asphalt underneath to accelerated UV breakdown.

Why Hail Damage Gets Missed

A roof that looks fine from the parking lot can carry real hail damage. We walk the entire roof surface, including the areas that aren't visible from an access point, checking membrane for bruising and granule loss, metal panels for dents and cracked seams, and flashing and pitch pans for impact damage that compromises a seal without an obvious crack. We photograph and mark each finding with enough context — location on the roof, size, and pattern — that the documentation holds up to review.

Test Squares and Impact Patterns

Adjusters and contractors often use marked test squares on a metal roof to count hail hits per square foot, which helps establish whether the damage pattern is consistent with a hail event versus scattered pre-existing wear. We use the same approach so our findings translate directly into terms an adjuster is already working with, rather than a separate system that has to be reconciled during review.

Distinguishing Hail Damage From Ordinary Wear

Not every dent or granule loss on an older roof is storm-related. Odessa's UV exposure and thermal cycling age roofing material on their own, and a roof that was already showing wear before a hailstorm can make it harder to separate what the storm caused from what was already there. We're straightforward about that distinction in our documentation, because a report that claims every imperfection as storm damage undermines its own credibility, and one that misses real hail damage costs the owner money they're entitled to recover.

Documenting for the Claim

  • Dated, location-tagged photos of every hail-affected area, including the less obvious spots
  • Test-square hit counts on metal panel roofs where applicable
  • Membrane bruising and granule-loss documentation on flat and low-slope systems
  • Notes distinguishing hail impact from pre-existing wear or prior repairs
  • A written summary suitable for review by your adjuster or your own representative

Odessa's Hail Exposure Isn't Occasional

This part of West Texas sits in a corridor that sees hail more years than not, and the building stock here reflects that history if you know what to look for. Exposed-fastener metal roofs on service yards and fabrication shops carry the scars of storms that hit years apart, layered on top of each other, which is exactly why a fresh hail claim on an older roof needs documentation that separates this storm's damage from what was already there. A roof that's taken three hailstorms in ten years doesn't read the same as a roof that just took its first one, and the claim should reflect that difference honestly.

Questions Owners Ask About Hail Claims

Does insurance cover hail damage to a metal roof?

Hail damage to a commercial roof is typically a covered peril, but coverage specifics depend on your policy. What we can tell you honestly is whether what we're seeing on the roof is consistent with a hail event.

How can hail damage a roof if there are no visible holes?

Bruised membrane and dented metal panels don't always puncture immediately. The damage weakens the material at the impact point, and that weakness can turn into a leak months later, which is why documenting it at the time of the storm matters even when the roof still looks watertight.

What if the hail damage is only on part of the roof?

We document the actual extent of the damage, whether that's isolated or roof-wide. Some hail events hit a roof unevenly depending on wind direction and hailstone size, and the documentation should reflect that pattern rather than assume uniform damage.

Can you tell hail damage apart from normal roof aging?

Yes, that's a core part of what we document — the impact pattern, location, and type of damage hail causes look different from gradual wear, and we note that distinction plainly rather than blur the two together.

Do I need a hail claim filed before you can inspect the roof?

No, we can inspect and document hail damage any time, and having that documentation in hand often makes filing the claim more straightforward.

We're your roofing contractor, not a public adjuster — we document and substantiate the roof damage so you and your adjuster work from an accurate scope.
